verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: A criticism on Rufinus’ Apology to Anastasius.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: His excuses for not coming to Rome are absurd.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: His parents are dead and the journey is easy.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: No one ever heard before of his being imprisoned or exiled for the faith.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: His confession of faith is unsatisfactory.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: No one asked him about the Trinity, but about Origen’s doctrines of the Resurrection, the origin of souls, and the salvability of Satan.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to the Resurrection and to Satan he is ambiguous.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to souls he professes ignorance.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The poor souls must be tormented by his barbarisms.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is not permitted to you to be ignorant of such a matter which all the churches know.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to translating the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν, it is not a question, but a charge that you unjustifiably altered the book.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Origen asserts Christ to be a creature, and maintains universal restitution.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Where has he contradicted this?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The question is, as Anastasius says to John of Jerusalem, with what motive you translated the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You pretend not to be Origen’s defender, but you publish and enlarge the Apology for him and allege the heretics’ falsification of his works.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Your defence gains no support from Eusebius or Didymus, who, each for his own reason, defend the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν as it stands.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If we may allege falsification at every turn we make a chaos of all past literature.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The object of Origen’s letter, of which he translates only a part, is not to shew the falsification of his writings but to vituperate the Bishops who condemned him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is only in reference to a particular point in his dispute with Candidus that Origen alleges this falsification.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The story of Hilary’s being condemned through his writings having been falsified has no foundation.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: That which you tell about myself in Damasus’ council is mere after-dinner gossip.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The attack on Epiphanius as a plagiarist of Origen is an outrage on the Bishops generally.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Origen never wrote 6000 books.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I ascertained at the library at Cæsarea that the Apology you quote as Pamphilus’ is the work of Eusebius.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The letter falsely circulated in Africa as mine, and expressing regret for my translation of the Old Test. from the Hebrew bears the mark of your hand.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I have always honoured the Seventy Translators.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In proof of this, I bring forward the prefaces to my Translation of the Books from Genesis to Isaiah.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to Daniel, it was necessary to point out that Bel and the Dragon, and similar stories were not found in the Hebrew.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: A vindication of the importance of the Hebrew Text of Scripture.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Though the LXX has been of great value, we should be grateful for fresh translations from the original.
